svg.text: QUECHUA
HISTORY & ANDEAN CIVILIZATIONS
QUECHUA ORIGINS
Proto-Quechua: central Peru highlands, ~500 BCE
Wari Empire (600-1000 CE) spread Quechua across Andes
1438-1533: Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu) adopted Quechua as lingua franca
Runasimi ("people-speech"): Quechua's own name for itself
Inca administrators required Quechua throughout the empire
INCA EMPIRE (TAWANTINSUYU)
Tawantinsuyu = "Four Quarters Together" — largest pre-Columbian state
Stretched 4,300 miles from Ecuador to central Chile
1533: Spanish conquest — Francisco Pizarro captures Atahualpa
Colonial use: missionaries used Quechua to spread Christianity
QUECHUA TODAY
~8-10M speakers; official in Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador
44 distinct Quechua varieties — not all mutually intelligible
English borrowings: puma, condor, llama, quinoa, coca
AYMARA HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
TIWANAKU CIVILIZATION
Tiwanaku (500-1000 CE): major Andean civilization at Lake Titicaca
Aymara likely spoken during Tiwanaku’s peak influence
Gateway of the Sun: iconic monument at Tiwanaku
Tiwanaku collapsed ~1000 CE — Aymara kingdoms arose
INCA CONQUEST OF AYMARA LANDS
1430s: Inca Viracocha subjugated Aymara kingdoms
Aymara nobles incorporated into Inca administration
Quechua overlaid on Aymara territory — bilingualism began
Lake Titicaca (3,812m): sacred to both Inca and Aymara
AYMARA TODAY
~2M speakers; official language of Bolivia alongside Quechua
Bolivia: first president of Aymara descent — Evo Morales (2006)
Aymara concepts: "suma qamana" (good living) in Bolivian constitution
ANDEAN LANGUAGE BOND
3000 years of coexistence in the Andes
Quechua spread through empire; Aymara preceded it
Both shaped by altitude, Andean cosmology, agricultural cycle
Pachamama (Quechua) and Pachamama (Aymara) — same Earth goddess
Two peoples, two languages, one mountain world
PLATE 46d ALT · QUECHUA / AYMARA · HISTORY
